ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet and sour pork; FOODON_03302051 (pork dish) used as the closest broader class anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Pork meat dish) is the closest defensible broader category; no specific sweet-and-sour pork code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for sweet and sour pork dish identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet and sour pork is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of battered deep-fried pork shoulder coated in sweet and sour sauce with pineapple chunks and capsicum slices.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and Sour Pork — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sweet and Sour Pork (咕咾肉). All per-100g values are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried pork with starchy coating and sweet sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour pork in the University of Sydney GI Database. The HPB-reported carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g is considered an underestimate for this dish, which contains starch batter and sweet-and-sour sauce with added sugars. A GI of 55 is considered a low GI value. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g, GL is recorded as 0.0 with the caveat that actual GL is likely higher due to underreported carbohydrate from batter and sauce. Consumers and clinicians should treat this dish as carrying a meaningful glycaemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweet and sour pork; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 2100 mg sodium, representing 91% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg, and the low potassium content (47 mg/100 g) provides no meaningful counter-regulatory offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Starch batter and added sugars in sweet-and-sour sauce drive rapid post-prandial glucose elevation despite underreported carbohydrate in the HPB record
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat this dish with caution: the deep-fried starch batter and sugar-laden sauce create a high glycaemic load in practice, and the HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0 g/100 g is likely a significant underestimate; request reduced sauce and limit portion to no more than 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100 g), deep-frying fat load, and cholesterol (89 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, sweet and sour pork is a high-concern dish due to its very high sodium content, deep-frying fat absorption, and cholesterol load; consumption should be infrequent and portion-controlled, with sauce served on the side to reduce sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); pork contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6 pro-inflammatory pathway)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise sweet and sour pork: deep-frying significantly elevates AGE content which worsens joint inflammation, pork shoulder is high in arachidonic acid amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and capsicum (bell pepper) is a nightshade that may aggravate symptoms in nightshade-sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Pork (咕咾肉, Gū Lǎo Ròu) is a classic Cantonese dish widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of pork shoulder coated in a starch-based batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a vibrant sweet-and-sour sauce made from vinegar, sugar, tomato ketchup or plum sauce, and sometimes soy sauce. Pineapple chunks and sliced capsicum (bell pepper) are stir-fried into the sauce, adding fruity sweetness, acidity, and colour contrast. The texture profile combines a crispy exterior with tender pork interior, balanced by the tangy-sweet sauce and the juicy bite of pineapple. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a very high 1050 mg/100 g. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100 g is considered an underestimate for this preparation, as the starch batter and sweet sauce contribute meaningful carbohydrates in practice. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (extreme sodium load), type 2 diabetes (starch batter and added sugars), and cardiovascular disease (sodium, fat, and cholesterol). It is not halal-certified due to pork content. The dish is a staple of Cantonese-style Chinese cuisine and is commonly ordered at lunch and dinner in Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The input record carries a confidence of 0.95 and review_status of 'reviewed'. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100 g is flagged as a likely underestimate for this preparation but is preserved as reported by HPB with a documented caveat.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB Singapore Foodo; this is considered a likely underestimate for sweet and sour pork given the starch batter coating and sugar-containing sweet-and-sour sauce. The value is preserved as reported per source fidelity rules, but consumers and clinicians should be aware that actual carbohydrate and sugar content is likely higher than recorded. Sugar_g is similarly reported as 0.0 by HPB; confirmed zero per HPB source record, though this is flagged as potentially underreported for this dish type. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB; plausible given the small quantity of vegetables relative to total dish weight. Saturated_fat_g of 3.2 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork shoulder deep-fried analogue (USDA SR Legacy #10006 adapted) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is an approximation. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific sweet and sour pork preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sweet and sour pork exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is not halal due to pork content. The Chinese name 香酥咕咾肉 used in the input record emphasises the crispy (香酥) variant; the canonical Chinese name 咕咾肉 is used as the primary local name.
